message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Excess death rates
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  during the spring of 2020 are computed in
                  <jats:italic>N</jats:italic>
                  = 540 level 3 European territorial units for statistics —NUTS3 in Belgium (40), France (96), Italy (110), Netherlands (44), Spain (50), Sweden(21) and United Kingdom (179)— from 2020 provisional week deaths, the population numbers for 2020, and observations in previous years (reference or baseline), all of them obtained from Eurostat web page.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>
                  Excess death rates are classified in three tiers. Largest 27 excess death rates (tier 1,
                  <jats:italic>E ></jats:italic>
                  1721
                  <jats:italic>×</jats:italic>
                  10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  ) were distributed exponentially with empirical complementary cumulative distribution function (empirical survival function)
                  <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  following
                  <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                  ∝ 2
                  <jats:sup>
                    −
                    <jats:italic>E/ε</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sup>
                  with
                  <jats:italic>ε</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>1</jats:sub>
                  = 958(42)
                  <jats:italic>×</jats:italic>
                  10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  . Tier 2 (the next 52 largest excess death rates,
                  <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                  < 1142
                  <jats:italic>×</jats:italic>
                  10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  also distributed exponentially with
                  <jats:italic>ε</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  = 379.5(89)
                  <jats:italic>×</jats:italic>
                  10
                  <jats:sup>−6</jats:sup>
                  . Tier 3 (smallest 460 excess death rates) were distributed normally.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:p>The results suggests that when, within some regions, the outbreak is above a threshold, interaction with neighbouring region become less relevant and the outcomes —excess death rates— become exponentially distributed as it happens with independent events.</jats:p>
title: Exponential distribution of large excess death rates in Europe during the COVID-19 outbreak in the spring of 2020

reference.unstructured: Janine Aaron , John Mullbauer , Charlie Giattino , and Hannah Ritchie , “A pandemic primer on excess mortality statistics and their comparability across countries,” (2020).
Annie Campbell and Edward Morgan , “Comparisons of all-cause mortality between European countries and regions : January to June 2020,” Office for National Statistics, 1–27 (2020).

reference.journal-title: “Scientific comment on “Tail risk of contagious diseases”,”

reference.unstructured: Jose Maria Martin-Olalla , “Age and sex disaggregation of crude excess deaths during the 2020 spring COVID-19 outbreak in Spain,” medRxiv, 2020.08.06.20169326 (2020).
